qa_pairs.question: How do you initiate a VNC server on a login node, including a timeout option?
qa_pairs.answer: You can start your vncserver on a login node by running `vncserver -idletimeout 86400`, which will set an idle timeout of 86400 seconds.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the `-idletimeout` option when starting a vncserver on a login node?
qa_pairs.answer: The `-idletimeout seconds` option ensures that your `vncserver` eventually terminates (once the specified seconds have elapsed with no VNC viewer connections) if you forget to terminate your vncviewer session by clicking `System -> Log out` in the VNC desktop.

qa_pairs.question: Which software is the `vncserver` command provided by StdEnv/2023 based on?
qa_pairs.answer: The `vncserver` command provided by StdEnv/2023 is based on TurboVNC.

qa_pairs.question: What is required the first time you launch a vncserver?
qa_pairs.answer: The first time you start vncserver, you will be required to set a password.

qa_pairs.question: For what purposes is the VNC server password necessary?
qa_pairs.answer: The password will be required to remotely connect to your desktop with a VNC client (such as vncviewer). The same password will be required when making multiple connections if you started your vncserver by appending the additional `-alwaysshared` option.

qa_pairs.question: How can you determine the TCP port your vncserver is listening on?
qa_pairs.answer: You can determine the TCP port your vncserver is listening on by running `grep -iE "\sport|kill"` on the log file, for example: `grep -iE "\sport|kill" /home/yourusername/.vnc/l4.nibi.sharcnet:1.log`. This will show the port, such as 5901.

qa_pairs.question: Will the vncserver continue to run after you exit the login node?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, the vncserver you started will continue running until the time limit you specified (with the `-idletimeout` option) is reached, even after you exit the login node.

qa_pairs.question: How do you establish an SSH tunnel from your desktop to the VNC server on a login node?
qa_pairs.answer: You can start an SSH tunnel from your desktop by using a command like `ssh nibi.computecanada.ca -L 5905:l4:5901`, which forwards an arbitrary local port (e.g., 5905) to the VNC server's listening port (e.g., 5901) on the login node (e.g., l4).

qa_pairs.question: What are the two ways to connect to the VNC server from your desktop once an SSH tunnel is established?
qa_pairs.answer: You can connect either by clicking the `TigerVNC Viewer` application icon and entering `localhost:5905` in the `VNC viewer: Connection details` popup window, or by opening another terminal and running the command `vncviewer localhost:5905`.

qa_pairs.question: What happens after you successfully enter the VNC authentication password?
qa_pairs.answer: After successfully entering the password, your remote desktop should immediately appear.

qa_pairs.question: Are there limitations for applications run on cluster login nodes?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, although there are no system time limits on the login nodes for processes, any applications you run in your remote desktop will be subject to memory and cputime limits.

qa_pairs.question: When is it advisable to start a VNC server on a cluster compute node instead of a login node?
qa_pairs.answer: It is advisable to start a VNC server on a cluster compute node if you require more memory, CPU resources, or GPU access for applications in your desktop, or for graphics acceleration, as these can be requested accordingly using the `salloc` command.

qa_pairs.question: Does starting a VNC server on a compute node automatically provide hardware-accelerated OpenGL capabilities?
qa_pairs.answer: No, while starting a VNC server on a compute node gives you dedicated access to a full graphical desktop, by default, it will not have hardware-accelerated OpenGL capabilities.
